attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a ten-year history of intermittent, generalised abdominal pain, bloating and loose stools. The pain is worse immediately prior to defecation, which then relieves the pain. There is no visible blood in her stools. She gets an episode of such pain about once a week and there do not appear to be any apparent triggers. Her sleep pattern is not disturbed by her symptoms. She has no other gastrointestinal symptoms, is otherwise in good health, does not take any regular medications and has no significant medical history. She consumes gluten regularly in her diet. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination is unremarkable with no tenderness or masses. Recent blood tests including full blood count, C-reactive protein, liver function tests, and tissue transglutaminase antibodies are all within normal ranges.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). Irritable bowel syndrome is a functional gastrointestinal disorder characterised by recurrent abdominal pain associated with defecation or a change in bowel habits. The Rome IV criteria for IBS include recurrent abdominal pain at least one day per week in the last three months, with symptom onset at least six months prior to diagnosis, associated with two or more of the following: pain related to defecation, change in stool frequency, or change in stool form. This patient's symptoms of pain relieved by defecation, bloating, and loose stools are highly consistent with IBS.

The absence of alarm features (such as weight loss, rectal bleeding, nocturnal symptoms, family history of colorectal cancer or inflammatory bowel disease) and normal blood tests further support this diagnosis. The chronicity of symptoms (ten years) without progression also makes a functional disorder more likely than an organic disease.

Crohn disease typically presents with more severe symptoms including diarrhoea (often with blood), abdominal pain, weight loss, and fatigue. Patients often have elevated inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein), anaemia, and may have extraintestinal manifestations. The normal blood tests and absence of alarm symptoms make Crohn disease unlikely in this case.

Coeliac disease can present with similar symptoms to IBS, including abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits. However, the negative tissue transglutaminase antibody test while the patient was consuming gluten makes coeliac disease unlikely. This serological test has high sensitivity and specificity for coeliac disease when performed while the patient is on a gluten-containing diet, as was the case with this patient.

Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) can cause bloating, abdominal discomfort, and diarrhoea. However, symptoms are typically postprandial and associated with specific food triggers. SIBO is often associated with predisposing conditions such as previous abdominal surgery, diabetes with autonomic neuropathy, or structural abnormalities of the bowel. The long-standing nature of symptoms without progression makes SIBO less likely.

Ulcerative colitis typically presents with bloody diarrhoea, tenesmus, and lower abdominal pain. Patients often have elevated inflammatory markers and may have extraintestinal manifestations. The absence of rectal bleeding, normal inflammatory markers, and the long duration of symptoms without progression make ulcerative colitis unlikely.
learningPoints: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is characterised by recurrent abdominal pain associated with defecation or change in bowel habits in the absence of organic disease.
Alarm features that warrant further investigation include nocturnal symptoms, weight loss, rectal bleeding, family history of colorectal cancer, and onset after age 50.
Normal blood tests including inflammatory markers and negative coeliac serology in a patient with typical IBS symptoms support the diagnosis.
references: Ford AC, Lacy BE, Talley NJ. Irritable Bowel Syndrome. N Engl J Med 2017; 376:2566-78.

Tye-Din J. Interpreting tests for coeliac disease: Tips, pitfalls and updates. Aust J Gen Pract. 2018 Jan-Feb;47(1-2):28-33. doi: 10.31128/AFP-10-17-4357. PMID: 29429314. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2018/january-february/interpreting-tests-for-coeliac-disease-1
